title: Hong Kong Criminal Law Relationship Graph
notes: Primary Hong Kong Criminal Law judgments were fetched from HKLII's public API.
Supplemental textbook passages were used to improve topic coverage and backfill missing authorities.
NotebookLM is optional in this build; uncovered topics are surfaced in monitor_report.json for later enrichment.
Curated lineage paths add Hong Kong and UK authority sequences with FLLW, APPD, DIST, and CODI edges.

members.label: Chan Wing-siu v R
members.note: Privy Council established parasitic accessory liability — foresight of possible harm sufficient for secondary liability in murder.

members.label: R v Powell and English
members.note: House of Lords confirmed Chan Wing-siu — foresight extends secondary murderer liability.

members.label: HKSAR v Chan Kam-shing
members.note: Hong Kong Court of Appeal applied Chan Wing-siu joint enterprise doctrine.

members.label: R v Jogee
members.note: UK Supreme Court repudiated Chan Wing-siu — mere foresight is evidence of intent, not a substitute. Requires intention to assist or encourage.

members.label: Ibrahim v R
members.note: Privy Council — confession must be free and voluntary; inadmissible if obtained by fear of prejudice or hope of advantage.

members.label: R v Wong Kam-ming
members.note: Hong Kong Privy Council applied Ibrahim voluntariness rule; voir dire procedure confirmed.

members.label: HKSAR v Lam Tat-ming
members.note: Court of Appeal reaffirmed Hong Kong approach to oppression and inducement.

members.label: HKSAR v Cheung Tze-keung
members.note: Application of voluntariness standard in complex conspiracy trial; judge's directions on voir dire.

members.label: DPP v Camplin
members.note: House of Lords — reasonable man test personalised by age and sex of accused.

members.label: R v Smith (Morgan)
members.note: Extended personalisation to include individual characteristics bearing on gravity of provocation.

members.label: Attorney General for Jersey v Holley
members.note: Privy Council nine-judge board — external standard restored; characteristics only relevant to gravity, not loss of control.

members.label: HKSAR v Liang Yaoqiang
members.note: Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal applied Holley standard to provocation defence.

members.label: R v Turnbull
members.note: Court of Appeal — mandatory judicial warning on dangers of mistaken identity evidence; ADVOKATE factors.

members.label: HKSAR v Shing Siu-ming
members.note: Hong Kong adopted Turnbull guidelines; warning required where conviction rests substantially on identification.

members.label: HKSAR v Wong Sau-ming
members.note: Court of Appeal considered quality of identification and adequacy of Turnbull direction.

members.label: HKSAR v So Wai-lun
members.note: Modern Hong Kong application extending Turnbull to CCTV and dock identification scenarios.

members.label: DPP v Smith
members.note: House of Lords — objective foresight of natural consequences as test for intention in murder.

members.label: R v Moloney
members.note: Corrected DPP v Smith — intention not the same as foresight of consequences; purpose or aim test.

members.label: R v Woollin
members.note: House of Lords — virtual certainty test: jury may find intention where result is virtually certain and accused foresaw it as such.

members.label: HKSAR v Kissel
members.note: Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal adopted Woollin virtual certainty approach for murder intention.

members.label: Sin Kam-wah v HKSAR
members.note: Further Hong Kong refinement on directions to jury on intent and foresight.

members.label: R v Lau Tak-ming
members.note: Hong Kong Court of Appeal — established sentencing tariff framework for drug trafficking offences.

members.label: Secretary for Justice v Cheng Chun-pong
members.note: Review of sentencing principles; prosecution appeal jurisdiction and appellate intervention threshold.

members.label: HKSAR v Ngo Van Nam
members.note: Court of Appeal issued tariff guidelines for drug trafficking — quantity-based starting points.

members.label: HKSAR v Lo Hau-wai
members.note: Application of totality principle and enhancement for organiser/leadership role.

members.label: R v Warner
members.note: House of Lords — possession requires knowledge that one has something; but not necessarily knowledge of its precise nature.

members.label: R v McNamara
members.note: Court of Appeal applied Warner — knowledge of substance in container sufficient for possession.

members.label: HKSAR v Abdallah
members.note: Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal — container doctrine; accused unaware of drugs in concealed compartment; knowledge presumption rebuttable.

members.label: HKSAR v Ching Kwong-yuen
members.note: Followed Abdallah container doctrine; prosecution must establish knowledge or wilful blindness.

members.label: Palmer v R
members.note: Privy Council — no duty to retreat; force used must be reasonable in the circumstances as genuinely believed.

members.label: R v Clegg
members.note: House of Lords — excessive force negates self-defence but does not reduce murder to manslaughter.

members.label: HKSAR v Chan Wai-man
members.note: Hong Kong adopted Palmer — jury must consider what accused genuinely believed and whether force was reasonable.

members.label: HKSAR v Yip Kwong-lam
members.note: Modern Hong Kong application; directions on instinctive reaction and proportionality.

lineages.title: Hearsay: Res Gestae and Modern Exceptions

members.label: R v Andrews
members.note: House of Lords — res gestae exception: statement admissible if made when declarant's mind dominated by event, leaving no room for concoction.

members.label: HKSAR v To Kun-sun
members.note: Hong Kong applied Andrews res gestae test; court cautioned against expansive use.

members.label: HKSAR v Tsang Wai-hung
members.note: Application of res gestae in domestic violence context; contemporaneity requirement assessed.

lineages.title: Money Laundering: Knowledge and Reasonable Grounds

members.label: HKSAR v Pang Hung-fai
members.note: Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal — mens rea under OSCO s.25(1): knowledge or belief, or having reasonable grounds to believe, that property represents proceeds of crime.

members.label: HKSAR v Yeung Ka-sing Carson
members.note: Applied Pang Hung-fai — subjective awareness and objective reasonable grounds test; no need to know exact predicate offence.

members.label: HKSAR v Doraiswamy Sinnasamy
members.note: Court of Appeal extended analysis to wilful blindness as equivalent to knowledge under OSCO.

members.label: R v Ghosh
members.note: Court of Appeal — two-stage test: (1) objectively dishonest by standards of reasonable and honest people; (2) accused knew it was dishonest.

members.label: HKSAR v Wai Yu-tsang
members.note: Privy Council applied Ghosh dishonesty test in Hong Kong fraud conspiracy context.

members.label: Ivey v Genting Casinos (UK) Ltd
members.note: UK Supreme Court — Ghosh second limb abolished; single objective test: what was accused's state of knowledge, then ask if that conduct was dishonest by ordinary standards.

members.label: HKSAR v Chan Chi-wai
members.note: Hong Kong courts post-Ivey; moved towards objective standard while acknowledging Ghosh remains technically binding in HK.

lineages.title: Bail: Risk Assessment and Grounds for Refusal

members.label: R v Vernege
members.note: Established common law grounds for refusing bail — flight risk, interference with witnesses, commission of further offences.

members.label: Re Kwan Kong
members.note: Hong Kong application of bail grounds; court balances liberty with risks.

members.label: HKSAR v Lai Chee-ying
members.note: NSL bail restrictions under Art.42(2) — presumption against bail for national security offences; higher threshold.

lineage_codes.FLLW: Followed or adopted in Hong Kong
